html: 1️⃣ What is Physical Synthesis? (Classroom Explanation)

text: Before we understand Physical Synthesis, let us first understand why it was invented.
html: Before we understand Physical Synthesis, let us first understand why it was invented.

text: When a student learns RTL and Logical Synthesis, the output we get is:
html: When a student learns RTL and Logical Synthesis, the output we get is:

text: Gate-level netlist  Logic is correct  Timing looks “okay” on paper
html:
Gate-level netlist
  Logic is correct
 Timing looks “okay” on paper

text: But silicon does not work on paper.
html: But silicon does not work on paper.

• Gates are placed physically
  • Wires have length, resistance, capacitance
  • Clock does not reach all flops at the same time
  • Power, congestion, noise start affecting timing

text: Logical synthesis assumes ideal wires  Physical world does NOT have ideal wires
html:
Logical synthesis assumes ideal wires
  Physical world does NOT have ideal wires

text: This gap created a huge problem in the industry.
html: This gap created a huge problem in the industry.

text: Physical Synthesis is the process of optimizing the design using physical information (placement, wire length, congestion, and timing) to meet power, performance, and area goals before final place-and-route.
html: Physical Synthesis is the process of optimizing the design using physical information (placement, wire length, congestion, and timing) to meet power, performance, and area goals before final place-and-route.

text: Let’s rewrite it in student-friendly English:
html: Let’s rewrite it in student-friendly English:

text: Physical Synthesis is where logic decisions are made by considering the real physical layout of the chip.
html: Physical Synthesis is where logic decisions are made by considering the real physical layout of the chip.
2️⃣ Why Physical Synthesis is Needed

text: “If logical synthesis already meets timing, why do we need physical synthesis?”
html: “If logical synthesis already meets timing, why do we need physical synthesis?”

• Does not know where gates will be placed
  • Does not know actual wire length
  • Uses estimated wire delay

• Wires become long
  • Timing breaks
  • Congestion increases
  • Power increases

text: “Fixing timing after placement is too late.”
html: “Fixing timing after placement is too late.”

text: Timing must be optimized with physical awareness  That is Physical Synthesis.
html:
Timing must be optimized with physical awareness
  That is Physical Synthesis.

• Logical Synthesis = Designing rooms on paper
  • Physical Design = Building the city
• Bedroom to kitchen looks close

• Bedroom is on 10th floor
  • Kitchen is in another building

• Longer stairs
  • Elevators
  • More power
  • More time

text: If you planned with city map knowledge earlier, design would be better.
html: If you planned with city map knowledge earlier, design would be better.

text: Physical Synthesis = Designing with city map in hand
html: Physical Synthesis = Designing with city map in hand

text: 4️⃣ What Exactly Happens in Physical Synthesis?
html: 4️⃣ What Exactly Happens in Physical Synthesis?

text: 1️⃣ Tool takes gate-level netlist 2️⃣ Tool performs initial placement (coarse) 3️⃣ Estimates:
html:
1️⃣ Tool takes gate-level netlist
 2️⃣ Tool performs initial placement (coarse)
3️⃣ Estimates:

• Wire delay
  • Congestion
  • Timing violations
    4️⃣ Performs     logic changes:
  • Buffer insertion
  • Gate resizing
  • Logic restructuring
    5️⃣ Re-analyzes timing using     physical estimates
     6️⃣ Iterates until timing improves

text: 5️⃣ Where Physical Synthesis Fits in the VLSI Flow
html: 5️⃣ Where Physical Synthesis Fits in the VLSI Flow
Physical Synthesis  ← YOU ARE HERE

• Physical Synthesis bridges logic and layout
  • Reduces late-stage surprises

text: You are a physical design engineer.
html: You are a physical design engineer.

• Too many ECOs
  • Schedule slips
  • Manager unhappy

• Timing improves early
  • Fewer ECOs
  • Cleaner layout
  • Better power

• Physical Synthesis uses physical information to optimize logic
  • Solves problems caused by wire delays and congestion
  • Reduces timing closure risk
  • Acts as a bridge between synthesis and place & route
  • Mandatory in modern deep sub-micron designs

text: “Don’t wait for the house to be built to fix the stairs—design them correctly before construction.”
html: “Don’t wait for the house to be built to fix the stairs—design them correctly before construction.”

•  Beginner engineers
  •  Industry-ready engineers
1️⃣ What is Physical Synthesis (Industry Definition)

text: In real semiconductor companies, physical synthesis is not defined in one line.
html: In real semiconductor companies, physical synthesis is not defined in one line.

text: Physical Synthesis is the process of optimizing an RTL or gate-level design using physical information such as placement, wire delay, congestion, and clock distribution to meet timing, power, and area goals before final place-and-route.
html: Physical Synthesis is the process of optimizing an RTL or gate-level design using physical information such as placement, wire delay, congestion, and clock distribution to meet timing, power, and area goals before final place-and-route.

text: Now let us break this into student-friendly meaning:
html: Now let us break this into student-friendly meaning:

• Logical synthesis thinks “What logic is needed?”
  • Physical synthesis thinks “Where will this logic sit physically on silicon?”

text: Physical synthesis changes logic decisions based on:
html: Physical synthesis changes logic decisions based on:

• Estimated placement
  • Estimated routing
  • Realistic wire delays

text: Important: Physical synthesis is not just optimization, It is optimization with physical awareness.
html:
Important:
Physical synthesis is not just optimization,
It is optimization with physical awareness.

text: 2️⃣ Why Logical Synthesis Is NOT Enough
html: 2️⃣ Why Logical Synthesis Is NOT Enough

text: This is the core reason physical synthesis exists.
html: This is the core reason physical synthesis exists.

• Zero wire length
  • No congestion
  • Perfect clock arrival
  • Ideal environment
🔹 What Happens in Real Silicon

• Gates are far apart
  • Wires are long
  • Clock skew exists
  • Routing adds delay

text: Logical synthesis cannot see this problem.
html: Logical synthesis cannot see this problem.
3️⃣ What Problems Physical Synthesis Solves

text: Physical synthesis was created to solve real silicon problems, not theory problems.
html: Physical synthesis was created to solve real silicon problems, not theory problems.

content:
Problem

Why it Happens

Timing failure

Long wires after placement

Congestion

Too many cells in one region

Excessive buffering

Fixes applied too late

High power

Over-buffering

Late ECOs

Fixing problems at routing stage

text: ✔ Timing degradation due to wire delay ✔ Congestion hotspots ✔ Excessive buffering ✔ Late-stage ECO explosion ✔ Power inefficiency
html:
✔ Timing degradation due to wire delay
✔ Congestion hotspots
✔ Excessive buffering
✔ Late-stage ECO explosion
✔ Power inefficiency

text: It fixes problems before they become disasters
html: It fixes problems before they become disasters

text: 4️⃣ Logical vs Physical Synthesis (Real Silicon View)
html: 4️⃣ Logical vs Physical Synthesis (Real Silicon View)

• Works on RTL
  • Uses wireload models
  • No idea where gates will be placed
  • Timing is estimated

• Uses placement-aware estimation
  • Considers real distances
  • Knows congestion regions
  • Timing is closer to silicon reality

content:
Feature

Logical Synthesis

Physical Synthesis

Wire delay

Estimated

Placement-based

Congestion

Ignored

Considered

Clock skew

Assumed ideal

Modeled

Timing accuracy

Medium

High

ECO count

High

Low

Silicon success

Risky

Safer

text: 5️⃣ Evolution of Synthesis (Why PS Was Introduced)
html: 5️⃣ Evolution of Synthesis (Why PS Was Introduced)

• Wires were short
  • Gate delay dominated
  • Logical synthesis was enough

• Wire delay > gate delay
  • Congestion dominates timing
  • Clock skew is critical
  • Power is extremely sensitive

• Placement-aware synthesis
  • Physical estimation early
  • Physical synthesis engines

text: Physical Synthesis was born out of necessity, not luxury
html: Physical Synthesis was born out of necessity, not luxury
6️⃣ Tools That Support Physical Synthesis

• Logical + physical awareness
  • Uses floorplan
  • Placement-driven optimization
  • Bridge between DC and P&R
• Early physical estimation is needed

• Full RTL → GDS flow
  • Unified synthesis + place + route
  • Strong physical synthesis engine
  • Industry-standard for advanced nodes

• Tight timing
  • Advanced technology nodes
  • Large SoCs

• Logical synthesis + physical guidance
  • Works with Innovus
  • Physical-aware optimization

text: 7️⃣ Role of a Physical Synthesis Engineer
html: 7️⃣ Role of a Physical Synthesis Engineer

text: This is very important for students.
html: This is very important for students.

• Analyze timing failures early
  • Optimize logic with physical context
  • Reduce congestion hotspots
  • Control buffering
  • Prepare clean netlist for P&R
  • Reduce ECO count later

• Understand timing deeply
  • Understand placement impact
  • Know when to restructure logic
  • Balance power, performance, and area

text: This role requires thinking, not just scripting.
html: This role requires thinking, not just scripting.

• Logical synthesis alone is not enough
  • Physical synthesis considers real silicon effects
  • It improves timing closure success
  • Reduces late-stage ECOs
  • Mandatory for advanced technology nodes

text: Physical synthesis teaches one core lesson:
html: Physical synthesis teaches one core lesson:

text: “Silicon does not care about theory — it cares about physics.”
html: “Silicon does not care about theory — it cares about physics.”

text: If you understand this, you are no longer a beginner.
html:
If you understand this,
you are no longer a beginner.

text: (What the tool needs to START Physical Synthesis)
html: (What the tool needs to START Physical Synthesis)

text: 0️⃣ Why Inputs Matter in Physical Synthesis
html: 0️⃣ Why Inputs Matter in Physical Synthesis

text: Before we discuss each input, understand one golden rule:
html: Before we discuss each input, understand one golden rule:

text: Physical synthesis cannot “guess” silicon reality — it needs correct inputs.
html: Physical synthesis cannot “guess” silicon reality — it needs correct inputs.

• Missing
  • Wrong
  • Incomplete

• Timing closure will fail
  • Power numbers will be wrong
  • P&R will suffer later

text: So inputs are the foundation of physical synthesis.
html: So inputs are the foundation of physical synthesis.
🔹 What Is a Gate-Level Netlist?

• A structural description of your design
  • Contains standard cells (AND, OR, DFF, MUX, etc.)
  • Created by logical synthesis
🔹 Why Physical Synthesis Needs It

• Does NOT work on RTL directly (mostly)
  • Needs exact gates that will be placed
  • Optimizes gate structure + placement impact

text: “Given these gates, how do I place and optimize them physically?”
html: “Given these gates, how do I place and optimize them physically?”

text: RTL = architectural drawing Gate netlist = actual bricks and beams
html:
RTL = architectural drawing
Gate netlist = actual bricks and beams

text: Physical synthesis is about arranging bricks, not drawing plans.
html: Physical synthesis is about arranging bricks, not drawing plans.

text: “What kind of silicon world am I building this design in?”
html: “What kind of silicon world am I building this design in?”

text: There are two main types used in physical synthesis:
html: There are two main types used in physical synthesis:

• .lib
  • .lef
3️⃣ .lib – Timing & Power Library

• Timing models
  • Power models
  • Functional behavior
  • Process corners (slow, fast, typical)

• Cell delay
  • Setup time
  • Hold time
  • Clock-to-Q
  • Transition delays

• Internal power
  • Switching power
  • Leakage power

• Boolean function
  • Pin direction
  • Timing arcs

• Calculate realistic timing
  • Choose faster or smaller cells
  • Balance power vs performance
• Replace AND2_X1 → AND2_X4 to fix timing

text: Without .lib, no timing analysis is possible.
html: Without .lib, no timing analysis is possible.
4️⃣ .lef – Physical Library Information

text: It contains physical data, not timing.
html: It contains physical data, not timing.

• Cell width & height
  • Pin locations
  • Metal layers
  • Routing tracks
  • Blockages
🔹 Why Physical Synthesis Needs LEF

• Estimate placement
  • Estimate wire length
  • Detect congestion
  • Understand routing feasibility

text: Timing depends on distance, not just logic.
html: Timing depends on distance, not just logic.

• .lib tells how fast a cell is
  • .lef tells how big a cell is

text: Physical synthesis is constraint-driven, not automatic magic.
html: Physical synthesis is constraint-driven, not automatic magic.

• Where things are allowed
  • Where things are forbidden

• Placement engine
  • Congestion control
  • Timing-aware spreading

• Chip boundary
  • Core area
  • Macro locations
  • IO positions
🔹 Why Physical Synthesis Needs Floorplan

• Understand distance between blocks
  • Predict routing paths
  • Perform placement-aware optimization
• Two flops far apart → timing issue predicted early

• Cells are not allowed
  • Routing is restricted

text: 🔹 Why They Matter in Physical Synthesis
html: 🔹 Why They Matter in Physical Synthesis

• Congestion
  • Detours in routing
  • Unexpected timing degradation

• Tries to avoid hotspots
  • Rebalances logic early

• Clock definitions
  • Input delays
  • Output delays
  • False paths
  • Multicycle paths
🔹 Why Physical Synthesis Needs SDC

• Only what is constrained
  • Only what matters for timing

text: No SDC → Tool does NOT know:
html: No SDC → Tool does NOT know:

• What is critical
  • What can be relaxed
9️⃣ Power Intent (UPF / CPF – Intro Level)

• Power domains
  • Power switches
  • Isolation cells
  • Retention registers
🔹 Why Physical Synthesis Needs It

• Place power cells correctly
  • Preserve isolation logic
  • Avoid illegal optimization
• Logic may break during low-power modes

text: You don’t master UPF now, but:
html: You don’t master UPF now, but:

text: You must know why it exists.
html: You must know why it exists.

• Wire resistance (R)
  • Wire capacitance (C)

text: 🔹 Why Physical Synthesis Needs Early Parasitics
html: 🔹 Why Physical Synthesis Needs Early Parasitics

• Tool estimates RC values
  • Predicts timing more accurately
  • Reduces surprises later

text: 1️⃣1️⃣ How All Inputs Work Together (Big Picture)
html: 1️⃣1️⃣ How All Inputs Work Together (Big Picture)

text: Think of physical synthesis like cooking:
html: Think of physical synthesis like cooking:

content:
Ingredient

Role

Netlist

What to cook

.lib

How fast it cooks

.lef

How big the pan is

SDC

Cooking time

Floorplan

Kitchen layout

Blockages

No-cook zones

Power intent

Gas / electricity rules

text: Physical synthesis does not start with commands. It starts with correct inputs.
html:
Physical synthesis does not start with commands.
It starts with correct inputs.

• Questions inputs
  • Validates inputs
  • Understands why each input exists
(VERY IMPORTANT – Students usually miss this)
0️⃣ Why This Module Is CRITICAL
“Timing is only about logic gates.”

text: That thinking is 100% wrong in modern VLSI.
html: That thinking is 100% wrong in modern VLSI.

• Logical synthesis timing looks good
  • Post-route timing fails

text: If students don’t understand this module:
html: If students don’t understand this module:

• They will never close timing
  • They will blame tools instead of physics

text: The synthesis tool understands real silicon effects like wire length, placement distance, congestion, and routing delay — not just logic equations.
html: The synthesis tool understands real silicon effects like wire length, placement distance, congestion, and routing delay — not just logic equations.

• Tool does NOT assume gates are next to each other
  • Tool predicts where gates will be placed
  • Tool estimates how long wires will be

content:
Logical Thinking

Physical Thinking

Zero wire delay

Real wire delay

Ideal placement

Congested placement

Gates are free

Gates occupy area

html: 2️⃣ Cell Delay vs Wire Delay

• Delay inside a standard cell
  • Given by .lib
  • Depends on:

• Input slew
  • Output load

• INV delay = 10 ps
  • AND delay = 25 ps

• Delay due to metal interconnect
  • Depends on:

• Wire length
  • Metal layer
  • Resistance (R)
  • Capacitance (C)

• Making gates faster doesn’t always fix timing
  • Shortening wires often helps more

• Short wires
  • Large gates
  • Cell delay dominated

• Long global wires
  • Thin metal
  • High resistance

• Logic delay: 35%
  • Wire delay: 65%

text: So optimizing only logic is not enough.
html: So optimizing only logic is not enough.

• R = resistance of wire
  • C = capacitance of wire

• Longer wire → more R
  • Wider coupling → more C
  • Congested routing → detours → more length

text: Every extra micron of wire adds delay
html: Every extra micron of wire adds delay

• Placement matters
  • Floorplan matters
  • Congestion matters

• Too many wires want to pass through same region
  • Routing resources become limited

• Longer routing paths
  • Higher RC delay
  • DRC violations
  • Timing degradation

• Predicts congestion early
  • Spreads logic
  • Avoids hot spots
  • Re-structures logic to reduce wiring

• Optimize logic first
  • Place later

• Place first (roughly)
  • Optimize based on placement

text: If two flops are far apart:
html: If two flops are far apart:

• Tool may insert buffer
  • Tool may restructure logic
  • Tool may move logic closer

• Tool estimates wire paths
  • Calculates approximate RC
  • Predicts timing more accurately

• Early timing fixes
  • Fewer surprises in P&R
  • Faster convergence

text: Timing is not just about gates — it is about distance.
html: Timing is not just about gates — it is about distance.

• Thinks in microns
  • Thinks in RC
  • Thinks in congestion
(Bridge Between Synthesis and P&R)

text: “Why does synthesis care about floorplan? Isn’t that P&R’s job?”
html: “Why does synthesis care about floorplan? Isn’t that P&R’s job?”

• Tool guesses distances
  • Timing predictions are wrong

• Chip boundary
  • Core region
  • Macro locations
  • IO locations
  • Routing regions

text: It is the physical blueprint of the chip.
html: It is the physical blueprint of the chip.
2️⃣ Die Area vs Core Area

• Total chip size
  • Includes IO ring and seal ring

• Region where standard cells are placed
  • Excludes IOs and pads

text: Physical synthesis optimizes inside the core.
html: Physical synthesis optimizes inside the core.
Width : Height of the core

• 1:1 (square)
  • 1.2:1
  • 2:1

• Congestion
  • Long wires
  • Poor timing

text: Physical synthesis must adapt logic based on shape.
html: Physical synthesis must adapt logic based on shape.

• 70% utilization → good
  • 85% utilization → risky
  • 95% utilization → nightmare

• Less routing space
  • More congestion
  • Timing failures

• Spread logic
  • Resize cells
  • Reduce density

• Entry and exit points of signals
  • Distance to internal logic

• Long wires
  • Input/output timing issues

text: Physical synthesis must know IO locations for:
html: Physical synthesis must know IO locations for:

• Large blocks (SRAM, PLL, IP)
  • Fixed size
  • Limited placement flexibility

• Block routing
  • Create congestion
  • Force detours

• Places logic around macros
  • Avoids critical paths crossing macros

text: 7️⃣ Why Physical Synthesis MUST Know Floorplan
html: 7️⃣ Why Physical Synthesis MUST Know Floorplan

• Wire delay is guessed
  • Congestion is ignored
  • Timing closure fails late

• Realistic timing
  • Better optimization
  • Faster P&R convergence

text: Floorplan awareness turns synthesis into silicon-aware engineering.
html: Floorplan awareness turns synthesis into silicon-aware engineering.

text: Good floorplan → good timing → good chip
html: Good floorplan → good timing → good chip
0️⃣ Why This Module Matters

text: If someone asks in an interview:
html: If someone asks in an interview:

• They understand what the tool is actually doing
  • They stop treating synthesis like a black box

text: 🔹 What is Translation in Physical Synthesis?
html: 🔹 What is Translation in Physical Synthesis?

text: Translation is the first step where the tool builds an internal physical model of the design.
html: Translation is the first step where the tool builds an internal physical model of the design.

• Gate-level netlist
  • Timing libraries (.lib)
  • Physical libraries (.lef)
  • Constraints (.sdc)
  • Floorplan info

• Logical connections are mapped to physical cells
  • Cells get:

• Width
  • Height
  • Pin locations
• Nets are associated with:
• Estimated wire models

text: Without this step, placement is impossible.
html: Without this step, placement is impossible.

• Rough placement
  • Timing-driven
  • Congestion-aware

• Spreads standard cells in core
  • Keeps connected logic close
  • Respects macros and blockages
  • Estimates wire lengths

text: This placement is good enough to:
html: This placement is good enough to:

• Estimate RC
  • Analyze timing realistically
3️⃣ Timing Analysis with Estimated RC
🔹 Why RC Estimation Is Needed

• Wire resistance
  • Wire capacitance

• Uses early routing models
  • Runs static timing analysis (STA)

text: Now timing is close to real silicon.
html: Now timing is close to real silicon.

text: This is where physical synthesis earns its name.
html: This is where physical synthesis earns its name.

text: The tool enters an iterative loop:
html: The tool enters an iterative loop:

• Cell sizes change
  • Buffers are added
  • Logic structure changes

• Moves cells
  • Reduces wire length
  • Improves congestion
  • Rebalances density

• Large RC delay
  • Slew degradation

• Break long nets
  • Improve transition
  • Fix setup/hold
• Small cell → bigger drive cell

• Bigger cell → faster
  • Bigger cell → more power + area

text: Tool decides based on timing criticality.
html: Tool decides based on timing criticality.

text: Changing logic structure without changing functionality.
html: Changing logic structure without changing functionality.

• Re-balancing logic levels
  • Re-factoring expressions
  • Re-ordering logic cones

• Setup violations = 0 (or acceptable)
  • Hold violations = 0
  • WNS/TNS within limits

• Timing is met
  • Power is acceptable
  • Area is reasonable

text: Placement-aware, timing-driven optimization in a loop
html: Placement-aware, timing-driven optimization in a loop

text: Timing is NOT fixed using one technique.
html: Timing is NOT fixed using one technique.

• Used for critical paths
  • Improves setup timing
  • Increases power

• Repeater insertion
  • Load isolation
  • Slew fixing

• Buffer exists
  • But placed badly

• Moves buffer closer to load
  • Reduces wire delay

• Deep logic levels
  • Unbalanced paths

• Redistributes logic
  • Improves path slack

• Functionally same
  • Different physical/timing characteristics
• Use fast, power-hungry cells
• Use slow, low-power cells

• Tool downsizes non-critical cells
  • Removes unnecessary buffers

text: Reduce area & power without breaking timing
html: Reduce area & power without breaking timing

• Exact placement
  • Net topology
  • Neighbor congestion

text: Optimizes physical path, not logical path.
html: Optimizes physical path, not logical path.

text: Balancing timing, power, and area using physical reality
html: Balancing timing, power, and area using physical reality

• Design meets timing
  • Across all conditions
  • With no violations

• Data arrives late
  • Before clock edge

• Long logic
  • Long wires
  • Slow cells

• Data arrives too early
  • After clock edge

• Short paths
  • Aggressive optimization
3️⃣ Why Timing Changes After Placement

• Wire delays appear
  • Congestion affects routing
  • Placement alters distances

text: That’s why logical timing ≠ physical timing.
html: That’s why logical timing ≠ physical timing.
• Tool fixes entire path

• Over-fixing
  • Creating new violations

• WNS ≥ 0
  • TNS = 0
  • FEP = 0

• Voltage
  • Temperature
  • Process

• SS, FF, TT
  • High temp, low temp

• Multiple modes (functional, test)
  • Multiple corners
• Close timing across all corners & modes

text: The final exam of physical synthesis
html: The final exam of physical synthesis

text: Only physically-aware optimization can pass it.
html: Only physically-aware optimization can pass it.

text: CLOCK NETWORK AWARENESS IN PHYSICAL SYNTHESIS
html: CLOCK NETWORK AWARENESS IN PHYSICAL SYNTHESIS

• Data can be wrong
  • Logic can be perfect
    If     clock is bad, the chip fails.

text: Clock network controls the entire chip behavior
html: Clock network controls the entire chip behavior

text: Physical synthesis must respect the clock, even before CTS.
html: Physical synthesis must respect the clock, even before CTS.

text: Time taken by clock signal to reach a flip-flop from clock source
html: Time taken by clock signal to reach a flip-flop from clock source
• Clock source → chip boundary
• Clock tree → flip-flop

text: Physical synthesis mainly worries about:  Estimated network latency
html:
Physical synthesis mainly worries about:
 Estimated network latency

• Setup violations increase
    If clock arrives early:
  • Hold violations increase

text: So PS must estimate clock arrival.
html: So PS must estimate clock arrival.

text: Difference in clock arrival time between two flip-flops
html: Difference in clock arrival time between two flip-flops

• FF1 clock arrives at 100ps
  • FF2 clock arrives at 160ps
    ➡️ Skew = 60ps

• Positive skew → setup issues
  • Negative skew → hold issues
Reduce skew impact before CTS

• Jitter
  • Variation
  • Modeling inaccuracy
• Added pessimism in timing

text: 🔹 Why PS Must Account for It
html: 🔹 Why PS Must Account for It
• Uncertainty can break timing later

text: Physical synthesis does NOT build the clock tree.
html: Physical synthesis does NOT build the clock tree.

• Estimates clock tree behavior
  • Uses virtual clock trees

• Approx clock depth
  • Approx buffer count
  • Approx latency

text: This helps avoid surprises after CTS.
html: This helps avoid surprises after CTS.

text: 5️⃣ Why Physical Synthesis Cares About Clock
html: 5️⃣ Why Physical Synthesis Cares About Clock

• Clock affects every path
  • Clock defines timing window
  • Clock failures = chip failure

text: Prepare design so CTS becomes easy
html: Prepare design so CTS becomes easy
6️⃣ CTS vs Physical Synthesis Responsibilities

content:
Physical Synthesis

CTS

Estimates clock

Builds real clock tree

Prepares design

Balances skew

Fixes logic timing

Fixes clock timing

Reduces risk

Finalizes clock

html:
• Fix long data paths
  • Reduce clock sensitivity
  • Balance logic depth

text: Avoid massive timing changes after CTS
html: Avoid massive timing changes after CTS

text: Respects clock early to avoid late-stage disasters
html: Respects clock early to avoid late-stage disasters

text: 0️⃣ Why Congestion Is a BIG Problem
html: 0️⃣ Why Congestion Is a BIG Problem
• Sees real routing resources ✔️

• Break timing
  • Increase power
  • Cause routing failure

text: Too many wires trying to pass through limited routing area
html: Too many wires trying to pass through limited routing area

• Small road
  • Too many vehicles
    ➡️ Traffic jam

• High utilization
  • Large macros close together
  • Poor placement
  • Too many connections in one area

• Near macros
  • Near clock roots
  • In dense logic regions

• Longer routes
  • Detours
  • Increased RC delay

• More buffers
  • Higher switching capacitance

• Router may fail
  • Design may not tape out

• Avoids placing too many cells in one region
  • Spreads logic intelligently

• Increase spacing between wires
  • Reduce coupling
  • Reduce congestion

• Reduce local density
  • Increase whitespace
  • Improve routability

text: Prevents routing and timing failures before they happen
html: Prevents routing and timing failures before they happen

• Power
  • Heat
  • Battery life

• Longer wires → more capacitance
  • Bigger cells → more power
• Even when circuit is idle

• Transistor leakage
  • Advanced nodes

• Estimated activity
  • Default assumptions

• PS estimates power roughly
  • Identifies power hotspots

• Avoid unnecessary buffers
  • Prefer low-leakage cells
  • Reduce switching on non-critical paths

• Clock gating
  • Power gating (handled later)
Delivers fast AND efficient silicon

text: (Students confuse this a LOT – so we go very deep)
html: (Students confuse this a LOT – so we go very deep)

text: From outside, both PS and P&R:
html: From outside, both PS and P&R:

• Touch placement
  • Talk about timing
  • Mention buffers, sizing, congestion
Logic optimization with physical awareness

• It does NOT do final placement
  • It does NOT do routing
  • It DOES change logic

• Sees estimated placement
  • Sees estimated wire delay
  • Fixes timing using logic changes

• Gate sizing
  • Buffer insertion
  • Logic restructuring
  • Path optimization
Physical realization of the design

• It places cells legally
  • It builds clock tree
  • It routes wires
  • It fixes remaining timing physically

• Exact cell locations
  • Real routing layers
  • Exact parasitics
  • Physical DRC rules
P&R changes geometry, not logic (mostly)

• Optimized gate-level netlist
  • Physical-aware constraints
  • Timing mostly clean

• A better design
  • A physically realistic design

  • Too many violations
  • Too many ECO loops

• Shorter critical paths
  • Better cell distribution
  • Fewer buffers later
  • Faster convergence

2️⃣ Timing Debugging Mindset (MOST IMPORTANT SKILL)

text: This is where average engineers fail and good engineers shine.
html: This is where average engineers fail and good engineers shine.
🔹 You Must Think Like This:

• Why is this path critical?
  • Is delay from logic or wire?
  • Can restructuring help?
  • Will CTS make this worse?

• Read timing reports calmly
  • Trace a path end-to-end
  • Decide what NOT to fix
